module_mood_contract: capable next step — student leaves thinking 'I can practice this on my own work,' not completion, not remediation; a clear next move with less live risk

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood lands — per phase and close, 8+/10 at every teaching beat.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Teaching beats score 8–9/10. Phase 4 scores 7 but is a transitional/setup beat (generate-again-to-set-up-comparison), not a teaching beat — mood-floor carve-out applies. Setup beats (preamble, Phase 4 re-run) scored null or 7 respectively. Close score 9/10.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood doesn't resolve early — M3 unease (if any) must not be resolved by a verification step that belongs to a later module.
rules_evaluated.evidence: M3's mood is 'capable next step,' not unease. The exercise delivers a repeatable method, not a completion ceremony. The honest callout at Phase 6 close ('even after this, the answer might still contain errors... human oversight is what keeps the operation truthful') deliberately withholds the 'done' feeling. Mood stays at capable-next-step, not resolved-and-finished.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Big Idea fidelity — file honors the module's Big Idea from the strategy doc.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Strategy doc M3 Big Idea: 'The live session gives the shape; the homework turns it into personal muscle memory.' The exercise builds exactly that: a repeatable source-checking loop in a personal folder, with CLAUDE.md persistence as the memory-transfer mechanism. The exercise's 'core method' in the maintainer block echoes the strategy doc's Homework 2 description ('verify a plausible Claude answer with two checks').

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Epistemic mood not punitive — unease is not-knowing, never having-done-wrong.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 2's teaching moment ('the useful result is often not "wrong." It is "not in the sources" or "too broad."') actively reframes the checker finding away from blame and toward epistemic precision. Phase 6 callout names the limits of the method honestly without assigning fault.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Lead with the discipline, not the failure mode.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Exercise title is 'Find the wrong claims' — technically names a failure mode. However the exercise body re-centers immediately on the discipline ('separate generator from checker'). The teaching moment in Phase 2 renames the finding from 'wrong' to 'not in the sources or too broad.' The operative discipline is source-grounded verification, not error-finding. N/A concern at title level; body-level passes.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 5 body: 'Watch whether Cowork actually runs this as a separate checker. The point is not the label; the point is that the checker reads the sources and the answer as its own job, then reports findings instead of rewriting.' This is the key teaching moment of Phase 5 — distinguishing a real subagent run from a label-only claim — but it lives entirely in body prose, not encoded in the prompt. A speed-runner who pastes prompt-to-prompt skips this body entirely and loses the verification discipline the phase is meant to land. The Phase 5 prompt doesn't ask the student to verify or confirm that the checker ran as an independent reader; it only asks for the comparison output. The teaching moment is structurally unreachable for ~30% of students.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add one line to the Phase 5 prompt asking the student (or Claude) to confirm whether the checker read the sources independently — e.g. 'After saving, confirm in chat: did the checker read the source files on its own, or summarize from what was already in context?'

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Audience tier determines authoring substrate — chat-level audiences use plain .md prompt files, not installed skills.
rules_evaluated.evidence: All prompts are fenced plain-text blocks the student pastes into Cowork. No skill installs required. Consistent with claude-basics audience tier.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Per-phase failure mode + escape hatch in maintainer block for every forcing function.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Exercise is at 'draft' quality state — maintainer block does not yet carry per-phase failure modes. This rule fires at sim-passed and above. Flagged as TODO for next pass.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Per-exercise leap test — three observable Monday-morning outcomes in maintainer block.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Maintainer block (lines 158–167) contains only 'Length' and 'Core method' meta. No leap test with three observable Monday-morning outcomes. The story judge blocks ship on missing or unfalsifiable leap tests per rule 45.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add to maintainer block: three named verbs the rollout custodian exhibits by next working day, e.g. 'opens a folder they know well, pastes the Phase 2 checker prompt, reads the findings without editing them first.'

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Body teaches the work; trainer manages the room. No authored conversation cues.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Exercise is self-study. No room-share commands, no compare-with-neighbor beats, no pause cues. Hard-grep phrase list ('tell the room', 'share with your neighbor', etc.) not present.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Session widget uses `**Session** *(verb, "name")*` source shape; placed by learning flow.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 13: `**Session** *(new, "Find wrong claims - first run")*` and line 85: `**Session** *(new, "Find wrong claims - second run")*`. Both follow the canonical shape. Mid-exercise re-fork placement at Phase 3/4 boundary is the correct canonical pattern (d) for a session that changes context.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Student agency — adults decide. Forced phases, enforced pauses read as paternalistic.
rules_evaluated.evidence: No enforced pauses. The exercise has a clean 'do not fix it yet' at Phase 1 close — this is a forcing function (gates the next phase's clean-slate teaching moment) not a scripted pause, consistent with rule 16's forcing-function carve-out.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This file is an exercise, not a lecture. Rule N/A.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Strategy-fidelity — verify this file doesn't front-run a downstream teaching moment.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Strategy doc M3 'Homework: build and verify' lists Homework 2 as 'Can you be trusted to run this? Take a plausible Claude answer, extract claims, map evidence, and run source support plus counter-evidence.' The exercise delivers exactly this scope. It does not reach into M4 (organiser runbook) territory. The CLAUDE.md persistence mechanic is M3's own contribution — not an M2 front-run, as M2 teaches the rollout crux synthesis, not the folder-memory mechanic.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Pattern-recognition LOs need n≥2 instances with contrast.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The exercise runs the fact-check on two different generated answers (source-answer.md via manual prompt, source-answer-2.md via the persisted-rule new session). The student sees the same source-checking pattern under two conditions: without the rule (Phase 2) and with the rule baked into CLAUDE.md (Phase 5). Contrast between the two runs is the explicit teaching beat at Phase 5. n≥2 contrast satisfied.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Heavy fan-out prompts get a body-side recovery callout.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Phase 2 prompt spawns one subagent checker — not a heavy fan-out (N>2 parallel, continuous polling, read-everything). No recovery callout needed. Phase 6 prompt asks for fix + one more subagent check — still a contained 2-agent sequence, not fan-out.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Pre-action framing doesn't preview the prompt's payoff.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Body prose before each prompt names the activity and context (what the phase is for) without revealing what the checker will find. Phase 2 body: 'Now split the work. The generator wrote the answer. A separate checker tests it against the sources.' Names the architecture of the move, not the finding. Phase 5 body names the question ('did the rule change anything?') which is fine as an anticipation frame, not a payoff preview — the actual comparison result is produced by running the prompt.

stealing_the_mood: Phase 4 scores 7 — setup beat (re-run the generation to set up Phase 5 comparison), mood-floor carve-out applies; not a teaching beat. No steal.
